result:
I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s afraid to leave. Not sunset exactly, more like the moment after, when the sky has already gone bruise-colored and the streetlights haven’t quite decided whether they’re needed yet. That’s the time I feel most like a ghost in my own life, when the outline of me is still visible but the details have been smudged away. I walk the dog and realize I’ve forgotten to bring a bag for her shit, so I circle back, hoping no one has noticed the small warm offering she left beside the fire hydrant. The dog doesn’t care; she’s already galloping ahead, nose deep in tomorrow’s stories. I envy her certainty that there will always be another smell worth investigating.

My grandmother used to say dusk was the hinge hour, when whatever you’d failed to finish could still swing either way—done or forgotten. She’d stand at the kitchen screen door, dishtowel over one shoulder, watching the barn swallow scissors of sky while the kettle shrieked behind her. She never rushed. She believed the day’s last light could rinse a person clean if you let it. I thought that was superstition until I got old enough to accumulate regrets sticky as sap. Now I stand at my own back door, plastic leash handle molded to my palm, trying to let indigo light scrub me raw. Most evenings it only manages to highlight the fingerprints on the glass.

Inside, the house makes the small sounds houses make when they think no one is listening: the refrigerator sighing like an old man settling into a chair, the living-room clock clearing its throat before the top-of-the-hour chime. I talk back sometimes—quiet thank-yous for keeping time, for keeping cold things cold. I used to be embarrassed by that, the way I address objects as if they might answer. Then the pandemic arrived and the list of safe conversational partners shrank to delivery drivers and the spider building a web in the corner of the window over the sink. I named her Evita because she kept waving one arm like a dictator mid-sermon. One morning she was gone, thread and all, and I felt the kind of loss that startles you with its disproportion. A spider. A silhouette. Erased.

I wonder how much of my memory is already scaffolding rather than brick. I tell people I grew up in a yellow farmhouse with a creek in the back, but the creek was actually two blocks away and the house was more beige than yellow once the sun bleached the siding. Still, “yellow farmhouse” is what I hand over, because it sounds like a place where someone could have been happy. Maybe happiness is just the story that keeps you moving forward. Maybe it’s the story that lets someone else imagine you. When my college roommate calls, she asks what I’ve been up to and I hear myself inventing a weekend: farmers’ market, new recipe, long run. None of it happened. What happened was I sat on the floor eating cold chow mein straight from the carton while watching a documentary about ships being broken apart on the beaches of Bangladesh. Men in T-shirts and flip-flops carried blowtorches the size of their arms, slicing the steel carcasses into sellable scars. I cried at the precision, at the way everything enormous can be reduced if you approach it corner by corner. Afterwards I folded the takeout box into a smaller and smaller square until it cut my palm.

There’s a scar now, a thin pale line shaped like the spine of a book no one bothered to write. I keep thinking I’ll press ink into it, turn the wound into a page. But I also like that it’s blank, a quiet margin in a body that rarely shuts up. My heart has been talking nonsense since 2020, skipping beats like a scratched CD, racing like it’s trying to outrun the future. The cardiologist says it’s benign, just electrical, as if electricity were ever harmless. She suggests less coffee, more sleep. I want to ask whether she’s noticed the planet doing the same arrhythmic dance—floods, droughts, fires, freezes—but the appointment is only fifteen minutes and she has already moved on to the next sqeaky chart. I leave with a printout of a Holter monitor stripe, my heartbeat translated into sawtooth mountains. I fold it into a paper airplane and sail it from the parking garage. It nosedives immediately, gets run over by a Lexus. I feel oddly seen.

Sometimes I picture every personal data point I’ve ever generated—location pings, late-night emoji choices, the time I googled “am I dying or just lonely”—as confetti swirling above the continent. A technicolor storm we collectively breathe in. We inhale each other’s curiosity, exhale targeted ads. I try to opt out, but even my toothbrush wants to sync with the cloud. I tell myself the cloud is just another name for sky, but the sky never asked for my cholesterol levels. I miss the dial-up era, that electronic duet of modem and line, the patience required. Whole afternoons could be spent waiting for a single image to unveil itself pixel by reluctant pixel. You had time to anticipate, to imagine what you were about to see. Now anticipation is a glitch. Now I scroll fast enough to outrun thought, yet thought keeps pace, tethered like Evita’s silk.

Last week I found a box of letters my mother wrote to her mother the year she turned nineteen—thin airmail envelopes, edges tinted red and blue like passport pages. She described the smell of Nairobi rain, a job typing invoices for a sisal company, a man named Francisco who taught her to dance the cha-cha and vanished one weekend without collecting his pay. Her sentences lift at the ends like kite tails, convinced the sky will catch them. I read them all in one sitting, the way you drink when you don’t trust the well tomorrow. When I looked up it was 2 a.m. and my phone was informing me I had 6% battery left, as if I too were running out. I turned it off, something I never do. The sudden darkness felt merciful, a velvet pocket sewn shut around me.

I keep returning to one line in her last letter: “I like who I am when no one here knows my childhood nickname.” I picture her at a wooden table, ceiling fan slugging the humid air, writing those words in loopy cursive that will eventually become my own handwriting. I want to travel back and tell her that forty years later I will still be trying on versions of myself in different towns, that the nickname will resurrect itself each time I visit home and my aunt calls it across the grocery-store parking lot. That the self is less a fixed star and more a deck of cards you shuffle every morning, sometimes yielding a hand you recognize, sometimes not. That liking who you are is a skill no app has managed to gamify yet, though probably someone in Silicon Valley is pitching investors right now.

Outside again, the dog circles the same patch of grass like she’s winding a tiny clock. Overhead, bats replace swallows, stitching the seam between night and almost-night. I think about the Bangladeshi workers falling asleep inside steel ribs, the spark of their torches still ghosting my retinas. I think about my mother’s letters, about the way she sealed each one with the tiniest dab of lipstick—pink evidence that she existed, that she wanted to be remembered vivid. I think about the scar on my palm, the spider who left without notice, the light that keeps forgetting to stay. The dog finally pees, trots away satisfied. I stand there longer than necessary, letting the first star burn a hole in the curtain, making room for whatever comes next.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
